q: Two electric dipoles of dipole moments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> are placed in two difference uniform electric fields of strengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The ratio of maximum torque experienced by the electric dipoles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: Two electric dipoles of dipole moments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> are placed in two difference uniform electric fields of strengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The ratio of maximum torque experienced by the electric dipoles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Maximum torque experienced by an electric dipole is τmax=PEsin90°.

So, τmax=PE

The ratio is τ1τ2=P1E1P2E2=1.2×10-30×5×1042·4×10-30×15×104=16
 

Hence, the value of x=6.

subject: Physics
q: Two identical charged particles each having a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are placed on a horizontal table with a separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> between them such that they stay in limited equilibrium. If the coefficient of friction between each particle and the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>, find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
question: Two identical charged particles each having a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are placed on a horizontal table with a separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> between them such that they stay in limited equilibrium. If the coefficient of friction between each particle and the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>, find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
solution:
Normal reaction applied by table on object will be, N=mg.

Both charges are same, hence a repulsive force will act between them.

Value of force acting will be, kQ2L2.

Now the maximum value of friction force which table can provide is,

=μN=μmg

For equilibrium, kQ2L2=μmg.

L=kQ2μmg=k0.25mgQ=2kmgQ

=29×10910×10-3×102×10-7=0.12 m=12 cm

subject: Physics
q: Consider a finite insulated, uncharged conductor placed near a finite positively charged conductor. The uncharged body must have a potential :
question: Consider a finite insulated, uncharged conductor placed near a finite positively charged conductor. The uncharged body must have a potential :
options: less than the charged conductor and more than at infinity.
more than the charged conductor and less than at infinity.
more than the charged conductor and more than at infinity.
less than the charged conductor and less than at infinity.
solution: The potential of uncharged body is less than that of the charged conductor and more than at infinity.

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $8 \, \mu C$ and $-2 \, \mu C$ are located at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm, respectively on the $x$-axis. The ratio of electric flux due to these charges through two spheres of radii $3$ cm and $5$ cm with their centers at the origin is _______.
question: Two point charges $8 \, \mu C$ and $-2 \, \mu C$ are located at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm, respectively on the $x$-axis. The ratio of electric flux due to these charges through two spheres of radii $3$ cm and $5$ cm with their centers at the origin is _______.
solution:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}$.

For the first sphere of radius $r_1 = 3$ cm centered at the origin, only the charge at $x = 2$ cm is enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 1} = 8 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_1 = \dfrac{8 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

For the second sphere of radius $r_2 = 5$ cm centered at the origin, both charges at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm are enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 2} = 8 \, \mu\text{C} - 2 \, \mu\text{C} = 6 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_2 = \dfrac{6 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

The ratio of the electric flux is:

$\dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} = \dfrac{8}{6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4 : 3$

q: An electric field is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The electric flux through a surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> lying in YZ-plane(in SI unit) is:
question: An electric field is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The electric flux through a surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> lying in YZ-plane(in SI unit) is:
solution:
Given:

E→=(6i^+5j^+3k^) N C-1

A→=30i^ m2

ϕ=E→·A→

⇒ϕ=(6i^+5j^+3k^)·(30i^)=180 SI unit

question: Shown in the figure is a shell made of a conductor. It has inner radius a and outer radius b, and carries charge Q . At its centre a dipole p→ is placed as shown then:

Electric field outside the shell is the same as that of a point charge at the centre of the shell.

Surface charge density on the inner surface is uniform and equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mo>(</mo><mstyle displaystyle="true"><mfrac><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mstyle><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math>.

question: The electric field in a region is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">E</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">E</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The flux of this field through a rectangular surface, area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Z</mi></math> plane is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mi>A</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>×</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>640</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

q: A parallel plate capacitor with plates of area <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> each, are at a separation of <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the electric field between the plates is <math> <mrow> <mn>100</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext> </mtext><mi>N</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the magnitude of charge on each plate is: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mtext>Take</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mo>∈</mo> <mn>0</mn> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>8.85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow> </msup> <mfrac> <mrow> <msup> <mi>C</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>N</mi><mtext>-</mtext><msup> <mi>m</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> <mo>)</mo></mrow></mrow> </math>
question: A parallel plate capacitor with plates of area <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> each, are at a separation of <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the electric field between the plates is <math> <mrow> <mn>100</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext> </mtext><mi>N</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the magnitude of charge on each plate is: <math> <mrow> <mrow><mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mtext>Take</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mo>∈</mo> <mn>0</mn> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>8.85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mn>10</mn></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow> </msup> <mfrac> <mrow> <msup> <mi>C</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>N</mi><mtext>-</mtext><msup> <mi>m</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> <mo>)</mo></mrow></mrow> </math>
solution:
E=σε0=QAε0=100 V/m

Q=100×A ε0

⇒Q=100×1×8.85×10-12C

=8.85×10-10C

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> has an initial velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . If an electric field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> act on the particle, its speed will double after a time
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> has an initial velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . If an electric field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> act on the particle, its speed will double after a time
solution:
Acceleration produced by electric field,
a=qE0mi^

After time t, velocity of the particle,
v=u+at

⇒v=v0j^+qE0tmi^ ∵u=v=v0j^

So, speed of the particle,
v=v02+qE0tm2

Now, given v=2v0after time t, so
2v0=v02+qE0tm2

⇒ 4v02=v02+qE0tm2

⇒ 3v02=qE0tm2

So, time interval, t=3mv0qE0

Here, we must note that no change in magnitude of velocity is caused by a perpendicular magnetic field. So, we are not taking effect of magnetic field while calculating change in speed.

subject: Physics
q: Two short electric dipoles $A$ and $B$ having dipole moment $p_1$ and $p_2$ respectively are placed with their axis mutually perpendicular as shown in the figure. The resultant electric field at a point $x$ is making an angle of $60°$ with the line joining points $O$ and $x$. The ratio of the dipole moments $p_2/p_1$ is _______.
question: Two short electric dipoles $A$ and $B$ having dipole moment $p_1$ and $p_2$ respectively are placed with their axis mutually perpendicular as shown in the figure. The resultant electric field at a point $x$ is making an angle of $60°$ with the line joining points $O$ and $x$. The ratio of the dipole moments $p_2/p_1$ is _______.
solution:
Let the distance of point $x$ from the origin $O$ be $r$.

Point $x$ lies on the axial line of dipole A. The electric field at $x$ due to dipole A is directed along the horizontal axis and its magnitude is given by:
$E_A = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{2p_1}{r^3}$

Point $x$ lies on the equatorial line of dipole B. The electric field at $x$ due to dipole B is directed perpendicular to the horizontal axis and its magnitude is given by:
$E_B = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{p_2}{r^3}$

The resultant electric field makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the line $Ox$ (the horizontal axis). Therefore, the tangent of this angle is the ratio of the perpendicular component of the electric field to the parallel component:
$\tan 60^{\circ} = \dfrac{E_B}{E_A}$

Substituting the expressions for $E_A$ and $E_B$:
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{p_2}{r^3}}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{2p_1}{r^3}}$

$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{p_2}{2p_1}$

$\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = 2\sqrt{3}$

Answer: $2\sqrt{3}$

subject: Physics
q: Two particles A and B having charges 20 μC and -5 μC respectively are held fixed with a separation of 5 cm At what position a third charged particle should be placed so that it does not experience a net electric force ?
question: Two particles A and B having charges 20 μC and -5 μC respectively are held fixed with a separation of 5 cm At what position a third charged particle should be placed so that it does not experience a net electric force ?
options: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> on the right side

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> on the left side of system

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> between two charges
solution:
Charge should be placed near -5μC at x distance

K(20)Q(5+x)2=K(5)QX2
4x2=(5+x)2
4x2=25+x2+10x
3x2-10x-25=0 ⇒x=5

subject: Physics
q: The electric potential as a function of $x, y$ is given by $V = 5(x^2 - y^2)$ V. The electric field at a point $(2, 3)$ m is __________ V/m.
question: The electric potential as a function of $x, y$ is given by $V = 5(x^2 - y^2)$ V. The electric field at a point $(2, 3)$ m is __________ V/m.
solution:
The electric field $\vec{E}$ is given by the negative gradient of the electric potential $V$:

$\vec{E} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} \hat{i} + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y} \hat{j} \right)$

Given $V = 5(x^2 - y^2)$, we find the partial derivatives:

$\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 10x$

$\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y} = -10y$

Substituting these into the expression for $\vec{E}$:

$\vec{E} = -(10x \hat{i} - 10y \hat{j}) = -10x \hat{i} + 10y \hat{j}$

At the point $(2, 3)$, substitute $x = 2$ and $y = 3$:

$\vec{E} = -10(2) \hat{i} + 10(3) \hat{j} = -20\hat{i} + 30\hat{j}$ V/m

Answer: $(-20\hat{i} + 30\hat{j})$

subject: Physics
q: An electron is moving under the influence of the electric field of a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> having surface charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>σ</mi></math>. The electron at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, if the electron strikes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow><mi>e</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____.
question: An electron is moving under the influence of the electric field of a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> having surface charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>σ</mi></math>. The electron at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, if the electron strikes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow><mi>e</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
The value of the electric field due to the uniformly charged plane sheet is given by

E=σ2ε0   ...1

Thus, the force on the electron is given by

F=-eE=-σe2ε0   ...2

So, the acceleration of the electron is given by

a=-σe2ε0 m

Given, u=1 m s-1, t=1 s and S=-1 m

Using the equation of motion, it can be written that

-1=1×1-12×σe2ε0 m×(1)2⇒σ=8ε0 me C m-2

∴α=8.

subject: Physics
q: The electric field in a region is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. The flux of the field through a rectangular surface parallel to $x-z$ plane is $6.0 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. The area of the surface is ________ $\mathrm{cm}^2$.
question: The electric field in a region is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. The flux of the field through a rectangular surface parallel to $x-z$ plane is $6.0 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. The area of the surface is ________ $\mathrm{cm}^2$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \phi=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times 10^3 \cdot \mathrm{~A} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & 6=4 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{~A}=1.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2 \\ & =15 \mathrm{~cm}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Six point charges are kept $60^{\circ}$ apart from each other on the circumference of a circle of radius $R$ as shown in figure. The net electric field at the center of the circle is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\epsilon_{0}$ is permittivity of free space)
question: Six point charges are kept $60^{\circ}$ apart from each other on the circumference of a circle of radius $R$ as shown in figure. The net electric field at the center of the circle is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\epsilon_{0}$ is permittivity of free space)
solution:
Six point charges equally spaced 60° apart on a circle of radius R, with alternating arrangement creating a dipole-like configuration.
Each charge creates an electric field at the center of magnitude $E_0 = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R^2}$ pointing radially (outward for +Q, inward for -Q).
By vector addition of field contributions from all six charges with proper angle consideration:
The charges at specific positions create a net field with components that depend on their arrangement.
For the given symmetric configuration, the net electric field is: $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R^2}(\sqrt{3}\hat{i} - \hat{j})$
This corresponds to magnitude $2E_0$ at angle of $-30°$ from the positive x-axis.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : If an electric dipole of dipole moment 30×10–5 C m is enclosed by a closed surface, the net flux coming out of the surface will be zero.

Reason R : Electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges.

In the light of above, statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : If an electric dipole of dipole moment 30×10–5 C m is enclosed by a closed surface, the net flux coming out of the surface will be zero.

Reason R : Electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges.

In the light of above, statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true and R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
An electric dipole contains two equal and opposite charges.

Thus, the net charge, as viewed from outside a surface enclosing the dipole, is zero.

The net flux enclosed by any closed surface is, then, given by

ϕ=qencε0=0

subject: Physics
q: Two charges $\mathrm{q}_1$ and $\mathrm{q}_2$ are separated by a distance of 30 cm. A third charge $q_3$ initially at ' C ' as shown in the figure, is moved along the circular path of radius 40 cm from C to D. If the difference in potential energy due to movement of $q_3$ from $C$ to $D$ is given by $\frac{\mathrm{q}_3 \mathrm{~K}}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}$, the value of K is :
question: Two charges $\mathrm{q}_1$ and $\mathrm{q}_2$ are separated by a distance of 30 cm. A third charge $q_3$ initially at ' C ' as shown in the figure, is moved along the circular path of radius 40 cm from C to D. If the difference in potential energy due to movement of $q_3$ from $C$ to $D$ is given by $\frac{\mathrm{q}_3 \mathrm{~K}}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}$, the value of K is :
solution:
Potential at C
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}_1}{0.4}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_2}{0.5}$
Potential at D
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}_1}{0.4}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_2}{0.1}$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)\left(\mathrm{q}_3\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{kq}_2}{0.1}-\frac{\mathrm{kq}_2}{0.5}\right)\left(\mathrm{q}_3\right)$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=8 \mathrm{kq}_2 \mathrm{q}_3=\frac{8 \mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{q}_3}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}$

subject: Physics
q: A point particle of charge $Q$ is located at $P$ along the axis of an electric dipole 1 at a distance $r$ as shown in the figure. The point P is also on the equatorial plane of a second electric dipole 2 at a distance r . The dipoles are made of opposite charge q separated by a distance $2 a$. For the charge particle at P not to experience any net force, which of the following correctly describes the situation?
question: A point particle of charge $Q$ is located at $P$ along the axis of an electric dipole 1 at a distance $r$ as shown in the figure. The point P is also on the equatorial plane of a second electric dipole 2 at a distance r . The dipoles are made of opposite charge q separated by a distance $2 a$. For the charge particle at P not to experience any net force, which of the following correctly describes the situation?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}=\frac{2 a}{\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ & \frac{4 a r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}=\frac{2 a}{\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ & \left(r^2-a^2\right)^2=2 r\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2} \\ & \left(1-\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)^2=2\left(1+\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)^{3 / 2} \\ & \left(1-x^2\right)^2=2\left(1+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}\left(x=\frac{a}{r}\right) \\ & \frac{\left(1-x^2\right)^2}{\left(1+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}=2 \end{aligned}$ Now for $\mathrm{X}=3$ We get $\frac{64}{10 \sqrt{10}} \approx 2 \Rightarrow \frac{a}{r} \approx 3$ [But for $a > r$ point charge will between the dipole where $\vec{E} \neq 0$]

subject: Physics
q:
In free space, a particle A of charge 1 μC is held fixed at point P . Another particle B of the same charge and mass 4 μg is kept at a distance of 1 mm from P. If B is released, then its velocity at a distance of 9 mm from P is:
[Take  14πϵ0=9×109 N m2 C-2 ]
question:
In free space, a particle A of charge 1 μC is held fixed at point P . Another particle B of the same charge and mass 4 μg is kept at a distance of 1 mm from P. If B is released, then its velocity at a distance of 9 mm from P is:
[Take  14πϵ0=9×109 N m2 C-2 ]
solution:
From conservation in mechanical energy:
-ΔP.E.=ΔK.E.
14πε01×1062110-3-19×10-3=12×4×10-6×V2
9×109×10-1289×10-3=2×10-6×V2
V2=4×106
V=2×103 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: The electrostatic potential on the surface of uniformly charged spherical shell of radius $\mathrm{R}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ is 120 V. The potential at the centre of shell, at a distance $\mathrm{r}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from centre, and at a distance $\mathrm{r}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the shell respectively, are :
question: The electrostatic potential on the surface of uniformly charged spherical shell of radius $\mathrm{R}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ is 120 V. The potential at the centre of shell, at a distance $\mathrm{r}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from centre, and at a distance $\mathrm{r}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the shell respectively, are :
solution:
Potential inside shell is equal to potential on surface
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{in}}=\mathrm{V}_{\text {surface }}=\frac{\mathrm{kQ}}{\mathrm{R}}=120 \mathrm{~V}$
at $\mathrm{r}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{kQ}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{120 \times 10}{15}=80 \mathrm{~V}$

q: Two insulating plates are both uniformly charged in such a way that the potential difference between them is $V_2-V_1=20 \mathrm{~V}$. (i.e. plate 2 is at a higher potential). The plates are separated by $d=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ and can be treated as infinitely large. An electron is released from rest on the inner surface of plate 1 . What is its speed when it hits plate $2 ?$ $\left(\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
question: Two insulating plates are both uniformly charged in such a way that the potential difference between them is $V_2-V_1=20 \mathrm{~V}$. (i.e. plate 2 is at a higher potential). The plates are separated by $d=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ and can be treated as infinitely large. An electron is released from rest on the inner surface of plate 1 . What is its speed when it hits plate $2 ?$ $\left(\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
solution:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\mathrm{eV}$
$\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}}}=2.65 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathrm{R}$ Assertion A : Work done in moving a test charge between two points inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero, no matter which path is chosen.
Reason $\mathrm{R}$ : Electrostatic potential inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is constant and is same as that on the surface of the shell. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathrm{R}$ Assertion A : Work done in moving a test charge between two points inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero, no matter which path is chosen.
Reason $\mathrm{R}$ : Electrostatic potential inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is constant and is same as that on the surface of the shell. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true and $\mathrm{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true but $\mathrm{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
solution:
For a uniformly charged spherical shell:
- The electric field inside the shell is zero everywhere (from Gauss's law)
* When the electric field is zero, no work is done in moving a charge
- Since work - force $\times$ displacement, and force - q̧E (where E - 0), work - 0
- The potential inside is constant and equal to the value at the surface
Assertion A is correct: Since the electric field is zero everywhere inside the shell, no work is done moving a charge between any two points, regardless of path.
Reason R is correct: The potential inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is indeed constant and equal to the value at the surface.
Does R explain $A$ ? Yes, because:
- Constant potential means zero electric field (since $\mathrm{E}=-\mathrm{FW}$)
- Zero electric field means no force on a test charge
- No force means no work done in moving the charge
Therefore, opxion (2) is correct: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q: Two thin wires rings each having a radius $R$ are placed at a distance $d$ apart with their axes coinciding. The charges on the two rings are $+q$ and $-q$. The potential difference between the centres of the two rings is
question: Two thin wires rings each having a radius $R$ are placed at a distance $d$ apart with their axes coinciding. The charges on the two rings are $+q$ and $-q$. The potential difference between the centres of the two rings is
solution:
$v_1=\frac{k q}{R}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{R^2+d^2}}$
$v_2=\frac{-k q}{R}+\frac{k q}{\sqrt{R^2+d^2}}$

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Total volume of the drop will remain unchanged,

So,  27×43πr3=43πR3

⇒R=3r

Potential of the small drops, 22=kqr

For bigger drop, potential will be

V=k27q3r=9kqr=9×22=198 V

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $2 q$ and $q$ are placed at vertex $A$ and centre of face $C D E F$ of the cube as shown in figure. The electric flux passing through the cube is :
question: Two point charges $2 q$ and $q$ are placed at vertex $A$ and centre of face $C D E F$ of the cube as shown in figure. The electric flux passing through the cube is :
solution:
Using Gauss's law:
$\phi = \frac{Q_{in}}{\epsilon_0}$

Charge $2q$ is at vertex A of the cube. A vertex is shared by 8 cubes, so the flux contribution from $2q$ through this cube is $\frac{2q}{8\epsilon_0} = \frac{q}{4\epsilon_0}$.

Charge $q$ is at the centre of face CDEF. A face center is shared by 2 cubes, so the flux contribution from $q$ through this cube is $\frac{q}{2\epsilon_0}$.

Total flux:
$\phi = \frac{q/4 + q/2}{\epsilon_0} = \frac{3q}{4\epsilon_0}$

subject: Physics
q: For a short dipole placed at origin $O$, the dipole moment $P$ is along $x$-axis, as shown in the figure. If the electric potential and electric field at A are $\mathrm{V}_0$ and $\mathrm{E}_0$, respectively, then the correct combination of the electric potential and electric field, respectively, at point $B$ on the $y$-axis is given by
question: For a short dipole placed at origin $O$, the dipole moment $P$ is along $x$-axis, as shown in the figure. If the electric potential and electric field at A are $\mathrm{V}_0$ and $\mathrm{E}_0$, respectively, then the correct combination of the electric potential and electric field, respectively, at point $B$ on the $y$-axis is given by
solution:
At point $A$ (axial)
$\left|E_0\right|=\frac{2 k_p}{r^3}, V_0=\frac{k_p}{r^2}$

At point $B$ (equatorial)
$\begin{aligned}
& |E|=\frac{k_p}{(2 r)^3}=\frac{E_0}{16} \\
& V=0
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two isolated metallic solid spheres of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> are charged such that both have same charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> . The spheres are then connected by a thin conducting wire. If the new charge density of the bigger sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>σ</mi></mfrac></math> is :
question: Two isolated metallic solid spheres of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> are charged such that both have same charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> . The spheres are then connected by a thin conducting wire. If the new charge density of the bigger sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>σ</mi></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
Charge and charge density on both isolated metallic solid spheres are show below.

Let the charges and the charge densities on both the spheres after connected by thin conducting wire be Q1', Q2', σ and σ' as shown below.

Once connected, charge on both the spheres will redistribute such that potentials at the surface of both spheres is the same.

∴kQ1'R=kQ2'2R

⇒Q1'4πε0R=Q2'4πε02R

∴Q2'=2Q1'

Using charge conservation, 

Q1'+Q2'=Q1+Q2

∴Q2'2+Q2'=20πR2σ

32Q2'=20πR2σ

∴Q2'4π2R2=23×20πR2σ16πR2

∴σ'σ=56
